dialogue.utterance: Unit 3, respond to the 7689 Sweeney Rd for a reported seizure.
Hi, I'm Emily, an EMT with Richmond Regional Rescue. What made you call 911 today?
I can't breathe. It's been getting worse since I got off work.
Ma'am, can you tell me your name and the date?
Okay, can you look at my finger? Right, left, up, and down?
Can you tell me when I say "Richmond"?
Please raise and lower your hand slowly.
Your airway is clear and patent. It's easier with you sitting upright.
I'm going to listen to your lungs now.
Respirations are at 28 per minute. They are labored.
I'm hearing wheezes at the apex, and no air movement in the bases.
Pulse is 113 and strong.
Trachea is midline.
Blood pressure is 144 over 92.
I don't see any abrasions, rashes, or signs of trauma.
Pupils are equal, round, and reactive to light.
Your nose and mouth are clear, and I see the nebulizer at the bedside.
Your chest is intact, with no visible pain or tenderness.
Your abdomen is soft, with no tenderness.
You have full use of your extremities without visible trauma.
You mentioned it worsened after work. Can you tell me more about when you first noticed it?
You also have a history of seizures, asthma, and migraines-have any of these conditions changed recently?
On a scale from 0 to 10, where 10 is the worst, how would you rate your shortness of breath?
Pulse Oximetry is at 99%-that's good oxygen saturation.
Blood glucose is 106.
The ECG shows sinus tachycardia but no new rhythm changes.
Let's begin nebulizer therapy with DuoNeb.
I'm going to give you Albuterol 5 mg and Atrovent 0.5 mg.
I will also administer 10 mg of Dexamethasone through your IV line.
I'm placing an 18 Gauge IV line in your arm for medications and fluids.
We'll apply four-lead ECG monitoring to track your heart rate.
We're following the respiratory distress protocol for asthma exacerbation.
Pulse has dropped to 79, and respirations are at 17 per minute.
Blood pressure is now 172 over 109.
EKG still shows sinus tach, but no new rhythm change.
Blood glucose is not registering.
Let's repeat the DuoNeb nebulizer treatment.
Second dose of Albuterol and Atrovent being delivered now.
Pulse Ox now reads 100%, so oxygen is holding.
Please keep the CPAP mask on. We need to maintain stabilization.
How's the breathing now on the 0 to 10 scale?
Earlier I asked about your usual asthma meds-are you currently taking Keppra or any other?
We're heading to Chippenham Hospital for specialized respiratory care.
We'll transfer you absolutely safely into the stretcher. We'll put you in semi-Fowler's position to help with breathing.
A full patient report and our medical notes are with the oncoming nurse.
She is alert, oriented, and interactive. You know who I am and what time it is.
Oxygen saturation is stable at 100%.
Airway is patent and self-maintained; no signs of obstruction.
You are alert, oriented, and interactive. You know who I am and what time it is.
What were you doing just before your symptoms began to worsen?
An 18 Gauge IV line has been established with Saline Lock for potential future use.
Temperature is not assessed due to immediate focus on airway and breathing.
Dexamethasone is administered to reduce inflammation in your airways.
Your breathing seems less labored, but there is still some wheezing.
Lung sounds are now showing wheezing in all fields, so your treatment is having some effect.
I've applied the mask for CPAP with 5 cm PEEP to help with oxygen delivery.
Have you been using any home care treatments for this prior to calling 911?
You mentioned you have no medication allergies, only seasonal allergies.
The medical team is aware you have asthma, seizures, and migraines.
Your heart rhythm remains sinus tach but there are no signs of deterioration.
Your breathing has improved with rate at 17, pulse at 79, and stable blood pressure.
No new oxygen requirements are necessary due to stable saturation.
The IV line is secured and in place for future medication administration if needed.
Your shortness of breath is reported as zero now-do you agree with that?
We'll transport you in the semi Fowler position for maximum comfort and airflow.
We've already contacted Chippenham Hospital ED bed 18 where you will be receiving care.
Your medical history includes Epilepsy, Asthma, Migraines, as well as seasonal allergies.
Five straps and raised rails ensure you're stable for transport while in semi-Fowler's position.
You're currently taking Albuterol, Atrovent, Keppra, HCTZ, and Dyazide, right?
We've delivered two nebulizer treatments and ensured you're still receiving CPAP support.
You maintain good saturation and stable vitals.
No changes in your use of prescribed medications before this event, correct?
Dexamethasone was through the IV, which helps your airways stay open.
Respiratory rate is steady at 17, though there is still some wheezing at the base.
Patient is secured on stretcher with all care items in order for transport.
Try not to remove the CPAP mask during transport-it's keeping your airway stable.
A three-person sheet draw will ensure safe transfer to the hospital bed on arrival.
She is medically A&O x4 with no change noted in baseline level of consciousness.
The IV remains a saline lock and not running at the moment-easily accessible for meds.
Please fasten your hospital transport belt once moved onto the stretcher.
We will continue to monitor you for any changes in condition during your transport.
The CPAP mask is remaining secure, and your breathing appears stable.
Passenger bar and safety restraints will be used for stretcher transport to the vehicle.
You're still reporting zero shortness of breath and feel much more comfortable now.
Vital signs have been recorded at 10-minute intervals and remain within normal ranges.
The staff at Chippenham Hospital is prepared to assist you as soon as we arrive.
No changes detected in breathing efforts or response to nebulization.
No new EKG findings indicating deterioration.
No signs of JVD or neck vein distention observed during transport.
No jugular venous distension noted.
